Algeria.name: Vladimir Petković
Algeria.playingCareer: A midfielder who spent most of his career in Switzerland with clubs including FK Sarajevo, FC Sion and AC Bellinzona; he did not earn senior international caps.
Algeria.managerialCareer: Managed Young Boys, Lazio (2012-13 Coppa Italia) and Bordeaux, and led Switzerland from 2014 to 2021 to the round of 16 at both Euro 2016 and the 2018 finals; took charge of Algeria in 2024.
Algeria.bio: Vladimir Petković is a Sarajevo-born coach who built his reputation in Switzerland before lifting the Coppa Italia with Lazio and guiding the Swiss national team through two major tournaments. He took the reins of Algeria in early 2024 and leads the Desert Foxes into the 2026 tournament.

Argentina.name: Lionel Scaloni
Argentina.playingCareer: A right-back who played for Newell's Old Boys, Estudiantes, Deportivo La Coruña, Lazio and Atalanta, winning 7 caps for Argentina including the 2006 finals.
Argentina.managerialCareer: As Argentina head coach since 2018 he has won the 2021 and 2024 Copa América, the 2022 Finalissima and the 2022 world title, and was named The Best men's coach in 2022.
Argentina.bio: Lionel Scaloni transformed Argentina from a side searching for identity into one of the most decorated national teams of the era. Since taking over in 2018 he has guided La Albiceleste to back-to-back Copa América crowns and the 2022 world title, and now leads them as defending champions into the 2026 tournament.

Australia.name: Tony Popovic
Australia.playingCareer: A centre-back who played for Sydney United, Sanfrecce Hiroshima, Crystal Palace and Sydney FC, earning 58 caps for Australia including the 2006 finals.
Australia.managerialCareer: Managed Western Sydney Wanderers to the 2014 AFC Champions League title and an A-League Premiership, plus honours with Perth Glory and Melbourne Victory, before taking charge of the Socceroos in 2024.
Australia.bio: Tony Popovic is a former Socceroos defender who became one of Australia's most successful club coaches, most notably steering Western Sydney Wanderers to a continental crown. Appointed national-team head coach in September 2024, he leads Australia into the 2026 tournament.

Austria.name: Ralf Rangnick
Austria.playingCareer: A defensive midfielder who played mostly in the German lower leagues for clubs such as VfB Stuttgart II and SSV Ulm 1846; he did not earn senior international caps.
Austria.managerialCareer: An influential tactician who built RB Leipzig and won the DFB-Pokal with Schalke 04, and served as Manchester United's interim manager; he has led Austria since 2022, qualifying for Euro 2024 and the 2026 finals.
Austria.bio: Ralf Rangnick is one of football's most influential modern thinkers, credited with shaping the high-pressing style behind the Red Bull club model and RB Leipzig's rise. Austria's head coach since 2022, he engineered the team's qualification for the 2026 tournament.

Belgium.name: Rudi Garcia
Belgium.playingCareer: An attacking midfielder who played for Lille, Caen and Martigues in France; he did not earn senior international caps.
Belgium.managerialCareer: Won Ligue 1 and the Coupe de France with Lille in 2011, and later managed Roma, Marseille, Lyon, Napoli and Al-Nassr; appointed Belgium head coach in January 2025.
Belgium.bio: Rudi Garcia made his name by leading an unfancied Lille to a domestic double in 2011 before managing some of Europe's biggest clubs across France and Italy. He took charge of Belgium's talented squad in January 2025 and guides the Red Devils into the 2026 tournament.

Bosnia and Herzegovina.name: Sergej Barbarez
Bosnia and Herzegovina.playingCareer: A forward who starred in Germany for Borussia Dortmund, Hamburger SV and Bayer Leverkusen, sharing the 2000-01 Bundesliga top-scorer award, and won 47 caps with 17 goals for Bosnia and Herzegovina.
Bosnia and Herzegovina.managerialCareer: Took charge of the Bosnia and Herzegovina national team in April 2024 and led the country to qualification for the 2026 finals, its first major tournament since 2014.
Bosnia and Herzegovina.bio: Sergej Barbarez was one of Bosnia and Herzegovina's finest attackers, a Bundesliga top scorer who captained his country. Appointed national-team coach in 2024, he guided his homeland back to the global stage by securing qualification for the 2026 tournament.

Brazil.name: Carlo Ancelotti
Brazil.playingCareer: A midfielder who won league and cup honours with Roma and AC Milan and earned 26 caps for Italy, finishing third at the 1990 finals.
Brazil.managerialCareer: A record five-time Champions League-winning manager who has lifted league titles in all of Europe's top five leagues with Milan, Chelsea, PSG, Bayern Munich and Real Madrid; he became Brazil's first foreign permanent head coach in May 2025.
Brazil.bio: Carlo Ancelotti is the most decorated coach in European club football, a record five-time Champions League winner who has conquered every major league on the continent. In May 2025 he made history as Brazil's first foreign permanent head coach, taking the five-time champions into the 2026 tournament.

Cabo Verde.name: Pedro Leitão Brito (Bubista)
Cabo Verde.playingCareer: A centre-back, known as Bubista, who played for clubs in Spain, Angola, Portugal and Cape Verde and earned 21 caps for the Cape Verde national team.
Cabo Verde.managerialCareer: Known as Bubista, he has led Cape Verde since 2020, reaching the 2023 Africa Cup of Nations quarter-finals and securing the nation's first-ever qualification for the 2026 finals; named 2025 CAF Coach of the Year.
Cabo Verde.bio: Pedro Leitão Brito, universally known as Bubista, is a former national-team defender who has become a symbol of Cape Verdean football's rise. As head coach since 2020 he guided the tiny island nation to its first-ever qualification for the 2026 tournament, earning CAF Coach of the Year honours.

Canada.name: Jesse Marsch
Canada.playingCareer: Midfielder who spent his career in MLS with D.C. United, Chicago Fire and Chivas USA, earning 2 caps for the United States.
Canada.managerialCareer: Managed New York Red Bulls (2015 Supporters' Shield, MLS Coach of the Year), won back-to-back Austrian doubles with Red Bull Salzburg, and coached RB Leipzig and Leeds United before taking over Canada in 2024 and reaching fourth at the 2024 Copa América.
Canada.bio: Jesse Marsch is a Wisconsin-born coach known for his high-energy pressing style honed in the Red Bull system. After spells at Salzburg, Leipzig and Leeds United, he took charge of Canada in 2024 and guided the co-hosts to a fourth-place finish at the 2024 Copa América.

Colombia.name: Néstor Lorenzo
Colombia.playingCareer: Defender who played for Argentinos Juniors, Bari, San Lorenzo and Boca Juniors, and won 13 caps for Argentina, featuring in the 1990 final.
Colombia.managerialCareer: Won the 2022 Apertura with FBC Melgar in Peru before taking charge of Colombia in 2022, leading them to the 2024 Copa América final and qualification for the 2026 tournament.
Colombia.bio: Néstor Lorenzo is an Argentine coach who, as a player, reached the 1990 final with Argentina. Since taking over Colombia in 2022 he has overseen a strong run that included reaching the 2024 Copa América final, and has guided Colombia to the 2026 finals.

Congo DR.name: Sébastien Desabre
Congo DR.playingCareer: Did not play professionally; moved into coaching early.
Congo DR.managerialCareer: A career manager who won silverware with ASEC Mimosas and Espérance de Tunis and led Uganda to the 2019 Africa Cup of Nations; took charge of DR Congo in 2022 and guided them to the 2026 finals.
Congo DR.bio: Sébastien Desabre is a French coach who has built his career largely in African football, with stints at ASEC Mimosas, Espérance de Tunis and the Uganda national team. He took over DR Congo in 2022 and steered the Leopards to qualification for the 2026 finals.

Croatia.name: Zlatko Dalić
Croatia.playingCareer: Defensive midfielder who played in the former Yugoslavia and Croatia for clubs including Hajduk Split, Varteks and Velež Mostar.
Croatia.managerialCareer: After club roles in Croatia and the Middle East (including a UAE Pro-League title with Al-Ain), he took over Croatia in 2017 and led them to the 2018 final and third place in 2022.
Croatia.bio: Zlatko Dalić is Croatia's most successful national-team coach, in charge since 2017. He guided the Vatreni to the 2018 final and a third-place finish in 2022, cementing his place in Croatian football history, and leads the side into the 2026 finals.

Curaçao.name: Dick Advocaat
Curaçao.playingCareer: Defensive midfielder who played in the Netherlands for ADO Den Haag, Roda JC, VVV-Venlo and Sparta Rotterdam, plus the Chicago Sting in the NASL.
Curaçao.managerialCareer: A vastly experienced manager who has led the Netherlands, Russia, PSV Eindhoven, Rangers and Zenit Saint Petersburg (2007-08 UEFA Cup); took charge of Curaçao in 2024.
Curaçao.bio: Dick Advocaat is a veteran Dutch coach nicknamed 'The Little General', with a decades-long career spanning the Netherlands, Russia, PSV, Rangers and Zenit. He took charge of Curaçao in 2024 and is set to become the oldest manager ever to lead a team at the finals.

Czechia.name: Miroslav Koubek
Czechia.playingCareer: Goalkeeper who played for VTJ Slaný, Poldi Kladno and AC Sparta Prague during the 1970s and early 1980s.
Czechia.managerialCareer: Long-serving Czech club coach who won the Czech First League with Viktoria Plzeň in 2014-15; appointed national-team head coach in December 2025.
Czechia.bio: Miroslav Koubek is a veteran Czech coach who won the domestic league with Viktoria Plzeň in 2014-15. A former goalkeeper, he was appointed head coach of the national team in December 2025 and is one of the oldest coaches at the tournament.

Côte d'Ivoire.name: Emerse Faé
Côte d'Ivoire.playingCareer: Midfielder who played for FC Nantes, Reading and OGC Nice, winning 41 caps for Ivory Coast between 2005 and 2012.
Côte d'Ivoire.managerialCareer: Promoted from assistant to lead Ivory Coast as caretaker during the 2023 Africa Cup of Nations, which the hosts won; named permanent head coach in 2024 and was CAF Coach of the Year 2024.
Côte d'Ivoire.bio: Emerse Faé is an Ivorian coach and former international midfielder who took over the Elephants mid-tournament and led them to a dramatic 2023 Africa Cup of Nations title on home soil. Confirmed as permanent head coach in 2024, he steered Ivory Coast to the 2026 finals.

Ecuador.name: Sebastián Beccacece
Ecuador.playingCareer: Did not play professionally; turned to coaching as a young man.
Ecuador.managerialCareer: Worked as Jorge Sampaoli's assistant before head-coaching roles at Defensa y Justicia, Racing Club and Elche; appointed Ecuador head coach in August 2024.
Ecuador.bio: Sebastián Beccacece is an Argentine coach who began as Jorge Sampaoli's assistant before building his own reputation in Argentine football with Defensa y Justicia and Racing Club. He took charge of Ecuador in August 2024 and led the side to the 2026 finals.

Egypt.name: Hossam Hassan
Egypt.playingCareer: Striker who is Egypt's all-time leading scorer with 69 goals in 177 caps (1985-2006), starring for Al Ahly and Zamalek and featuring at the 1990 finals.
Egypt.managerialCareer: Has coached numerous Egyptian clubs including Zamalek, Al Masry and Pyramids FC, plus the Jordan national team, before taking the Egypt job.
Egypt.bio: Hossam Hassan is an Egyptian football legend, his country's record international scorer, who took charge of the Pharaohs in 2024. A fiery, passionate figure who represented Egypt at major tournaments as a player, he now leads them into the 2026 tournament.

England.name: Thomas Tuchel
England.playingCareer: Central defender whose modest playing career with Stuttgarter Kickers and SSV Ulm was ended by a knee injury at 25; he earned no senior international caps.
England.managerialCareer: Acclaimed coach of Mainz, Borussia Dortmund, Paris Saint-Germain, Chelsea and Bayern Munich, winning the 2021 Champions League with Chelsea plus league titles in France and Germany.
England.bio: Thomas Tuchel is a German tactician who became England manager in January 2025, the third foreign coach to hold the role. A Champions League winner with Chelsea and a serial title-winner across Europe, he is tasked with ending England's long wait for a major trophy at the 2026 tournament.

France.name: Didier Deschamps
France.playingCareer: Defensive midfielder and France captain with 103 caps, who lifted the 1998 title and Euro 2000 and won two Champions Leagues at Marseille and Juventus.
France.managerialCareer: Managed Monaco, Juventus and Marseille before taking charge of France in 2012, leading them to the 2018 title, the 2022 final and the 2020-21 Nations League.
France.bio: Didier Deschamps is a French icon who won the 1998 trophy as captain and the 2018 edition as manager, joining a rare club to triumph as both player and coach. In charge of France since 2012, he leads them into the 2026 tournament.

Germany.name: Julian Nagelsmann
Germany.playingCareer: A centre-back whose playing career at 1860 Munich II and FC Augsburg was cut short by injury before any senior competitive appearances.
Germany.managerialCareer: Rose rapidly through Hoffenheim, RB Leipzig and Bayern Munich, winning the 2021-22 Bundesliga, before being appointed Germany head coach in 2023.
Germany.bio: Julian Nagelsmann is a young, highly regarded German coach who took charge of his national team in 2023 after a meteoric rise through the Bundesliga. Once the youngest head coach in Bundesliga history, he leads Germany into the 2026 tournament.

Ghana.name: Carlos Queiroz
Ghana.playingCareer: Did not play professionally at a notable level; moved into coaching early.
Ghana.managerialCareer: Vastly experienced manager of Sporting CP, Real Madrid, Portugal, Iran, Colombia, Egypt and others, and a two-time FIFA U-20 world champion; assistant for Manchester United's 2008 Champions League win.
Ghana.bio: Carlos Queiroz is a globe-trotting Portuguese coach appointed by Ghana in April 2026 ahead of the finals. With decades of experience leading national teams across four continents, he brings deep tournament pedigree to the Black Stars for the 2026 tournament.

Haiti.name: Sébastien Migné
Haiti.playingCareer: Midfielder who played at lower-league level for clubs including La Roche VF and Leyton Orient between 1989 and 2002.
Haiti.managerialCareer: Has managed several African national teams including Congo, Kenya and Equatorial Guinea, and was an assistant with Cameroon before taking over Haiti in 2024.
Haiti.bio: Sébastien Migné is a French coach with extensive African national-team experience who took charge of Haiti in 2024. He guided the Caribbean side to qualification for the 2026 tournament, only their second-ever appearance at the finals.

IR Iran.name: Amir Ghalenoei
IR Iran.playingCareer: Midfielder who earned 20 caps for Iran (1985-1996) and played for clubs including Esteghlal and Al-Sadd.
IR Iran.managerialCareer: Long-serving Iranian club coach with multiple Pro League titles at Esteghlal and Sepahan; led Iran in 2006-07 and again from 2023, winning the 2023 CAFA Nations Cup.
IR Iran.bio: Amir Ghalenoei is a vastly experienced Iranian coach who returned to lead the national team in 2023. A former Iran international and serial title-winner in the domestic league, he steers Team Melli into the 2026 tournament after a strong qualifying campaign.

Iraq.name: Graham Arnold
Iraq.playingCareer: Forward who won 56 caps and scored 19 goals for Australia (1985-1997), with club spells at Roda JC, NAC Breda and Sanfrecce Hiroshima.
Iraq.managerialCareer: Coached Central Coast Mariners and Sydney FC to A-League honours and managed Australia from 2018 to 2024 before taking the Iraq job in 2025.
Iraq.bio: Graham Arnold is an Australian coach who took charge of Iraq in May 2025 and steered them to qualification for the 2026 tournament, their first appearance at the finals since 1986. A former Socceroos striker and long-serving Australia manager, he brings major-tournament know-how to the Lions of Mesopotamia.

Japan.name: Hajime Moriyasu
Japan.playingCareer: Midfielder who spent most of his career at Sanfrecce Hiroshima and also played for Kyoto Purple Sanga and Vegalta Sendai, earning 35 caps for Japan.
Japan.managerialCareer: Won three J1 League titles with Sanfrecce Hiroshima and led Japan to the round of 16 at the 2022 finals and the 2019 Asian Cup final, earning AFC Men's Coach of the Year in 2022.
Japan.bio: Hajime Moriyasu is Japan's longest-serving men's national team manager, in charge since 2018. A former Sanfrecce Hiroshima midfielder turned title-winning club coach, he guided Japan past Germany and Spain to the knockout rounds and into the 2026 tournament.

Jordan.name: Jamal Sellami
Jordan.playingCareer: Midfielder for Raja Casablanca and Beşiktaş among others, earning 38 caps for Morocco and appearing at the 1998 finals.
Jordan.managerialCareer: Won the 2018 African Nations Championship with Morocco's A' team and the Botola Pro title with Raja Casablanca, then led Jordan to its first-ever qualification for the senior finals and to the 2025 Arab Cup final.
Jordan.bio: Jamal Sellami is the Moroccan coach who made history with Jordan, steering the side to its first appearance at the senior finals. A former Morocco international midfielder, he built a disciplined, well-organized team and was granted Jordanian citizenship in 2025.

Korea Republic.name: Hong Myung-bo
Korea Republic.playingCareer: Legendary sweeper who starred for Pohang Steelers and played in Japan and the US, winning 136 caps for South Korea and captaining the 2002 semi-finalists.
Korea Republic.managerialCareer: Won back-to-back K League 1 titles with Ulsan HD (2022, 2023) and took bronze with the U-23 side at the 2012 Olympics before returning to lead the senior national team into the 2026 tournament.
Korea Republic.bio: Hong Myung-bo is a South Korean football icon, a four-time finals player who captained the side to a famous semi-final run in 2002. After a championship-winning club career with Ulsan, he returned as senior team manager in 2024, carrying enormous national reverence into the 2026 finals.

Mexico.name: Javier Aguirre
Mexico.playingCareer: Combative midfielder for Club América and Atlante among others, earning 59 caps and 14 goals for Mexico across the 1980s and early 1990s.
Mexico.managerialCareer: In his third spell with Mexico he has won the 2025 Gold Cup and 2024-25 Nations League; earlier honours include leading Mexico at two prior finals and managing Atlético Madrid, Espanyol, Osasuna, Japan and Egypt.
Mexico.bio: Nicknamed 'El Vasco', Javier Aguirre is one of Mexico's most experienced and well-travelled managers, back for a third stint with the national team. The veteran tactician has coached across Spain, the Middle East, Japan and Egypt, and as co-host, Mexico carries high expectations into the 2026 finals under his guidance.

Morocco.name: Mohamed Ouahbi
Morocco.managerialCareer: Came up through the Anderlecht youth academy before coaching Morocco's U-20s to a historic 2025 U-20 world title (beating Argentina 2-0 in the final), then was promoted to the senior national team in March 2026.
Morocco.bio: Mohamed Ouahbi is the Belgian-born Moroccan coach who took charge of Morocco's senior side in March 2026. A product of Anderlecht's youth system, he made his name guiding Morocco's under-20s to their first global title, and now leads a talented Moroccan generation into the 2026 finals.

Netherlands.name: Ronald Koeman
Netherlands.playingCareer: One of the great attacking defenders, scoring prolifically for PSV Eindhoven and Barcelona and winning Euro 1988 across 78 caps for the Netherlands.
Netherlands.managerialCareer: Has managed Ajax, PSV, Valencia, Benfica, Southampton, Everton and Barcelona (2021 Copa del Rey), and is in his second spell as Netherlands manager, having qualified the side for the 2026 finals.
Netherlands.bio: Ronald Koeman is a Dutch footballing legend, remembered for his Euro 1988 triumph and a glittering playing career at Barcelona and PSV. Now in his second spell as national team manager, the experienced coach has led the Netherlands back to the global stage for the 2026 campaign.

New Zealand.name: Darren Bazeley
New Zealand.playingCareer: Right-back/right midfielder who made nearly 300 appearances for Watford and also played for Wolves and Walsall before finishing his career in New Zealand.
New Zealand.managerialCareer: Worked through New Zealand's youth ranks before taking the senior job, winning the 2024 OFC Nations Cup and qualifying the All Whites for the 2026 finals.
New Zealand.bio: Darren Bazeley is the English-born coach who guided New Zealand back to the global stage for the first time since 2010. A former Watford full-back, he settled in New Zealand and rose through its national youth setup before taking the senior reins in 2022.

Norway.name: Ståle Solbakken
Norway.playingCareer: Midfielder for Lillestrøm and FC Copenhagen who also had a spell at Wimbledon, earning 58 caps and 9 goals for Norway.
Norway.managerialCareer: Won eight Danish Superliga titles across two spells at FC Copenhagen and also managed 1. FC Köln and Wolverhampton Wanderers before guiding Norway to qualification for the 2026 finals.
Norway.bio: Ståle Solbakken is a hugely successful Norwegian manager, famed for a trophy-laden tenure at FC Copenhagen. National team boss since 2020, he has built his sides around a generation including Erling Haaland and Martin Ødegaard, and has now ended Norway's long absence by reaching the 2026 finals.

Panama.name: Thomas Christiansen
Panama.playingCareer: Striker who came through Barcelona's academy and played for clubs including Villarreal, VfL Bochum and Hannover 96, earning 2 caps for Spain.
Panama.managerialCareer: Managed AEK Larnaca, APOEL (Cypriot league title), Leeds United and Union SG before taking over Panama in 2020, leading them to the 2023 CONCACAF Gold Cup final and the 2024-25 Nations League final.
Panama.bio: Thomas Christiansen is a Danish-Spanish former striker who has transformed Panama into one of CONCACAF's most competitive sides. Since taking charge in 2020 he has guided them to a Gold Cup final and a Nations League final, and now leads them at the 2026 tournament.

Paraguay.name: Gustavo Alfaro
Paraguay.playingCareer: Midfielder who spent his playing career with hometown club Atlético de Rafaela in Argentina.
Paraguay.managerialCareer: A veteran Argentine coach who managed Arsenal de Sarandí (2007 Copa Sudamericana) and Boca Juniors (2019-20 league title), then coached Ecuador at the 2022 finals and Costa Rica before taking over Paraguay.
Paraguay.bio: Gustavo Alfaro is a much-travelled Argentine tactician known for organising competitive sides. After leading Ecuador to the previous finals, he took charge of Paraguay in 2024 and steered them back to the 2026 tournament.

Portugal.name: Roberto Martínez
Portugal.playingCareer: Defensive midfielder who spent most of his career in England with Wigan Athletic and Swansea City after starting at Real Zaragoza.
Portugal.managerialCareer: Managed Swansea, Wigan (2013 FA Cup) and Everton, then led Belgium to third place at the 2018 finals before taking over Portugal in 2023 and winning the 2024-25 UEFA Nations League.
Portugal.bio: Roberto Martínez is a Spanish manager who built his reputation in English football before guiding Belgium's golden generation. Now in charge of Portugal, he won the 2024-25 Nations League and leads a star-studded squad into the 2026 tournament.

Qatar.name: Julen Lopetegui
Qatar.playingCareer: Goalkeeper who featured for Real Madrid, Barcelona and Rayo Vallecano, earning 1 cap for Spain in 1994.
Qatar.managerialCareer: Managed Porto, the Spain senior team, Real Madrid, Sevilla (2019-20 UEFA Europa League), Wolves and West Ham before being appointed Qatar head coach in 2025.
Qatar.bio: Julen Lopetegui is an experienced Spanish coach who won the Europa League with Sevilla and led both Spain and Real Madrid. He took charge of Qatar in 2025 and guides the side at the 2026 tournament.

Saudi Arabia.name: Georgios Donis
Saudi Arabia.playingCareer: Midfielder who played for Panathinaikos, Blackburn Rovers and AEK Athens, earning 24 caps and 5 goals for Greece.
Saudi Arabia.managerialCareer: Greek coach who managed APOEL (Cypriot league and cup double), Al-Hilal (2015 King Cup) and Panathinaikos, before being appointed Saudi Arabia head coach in April 2026.
Saudi Arabia.bio: Georgios Donis is a Greek former international midfielder who took charge of Saudi Arabia in April 2026, stepping in shortly before the finals. With prior Gulf experience from a spell at Al-Hilal, he leads the Green Falcons into the 2026 tournament.

Scotland.name: Steve Clarke
Scotland.playingCareer: Right-back who made over 300 appearances for Chelsea (FA Cup and Cup Winners' Cup) after starting at St Mirren, and earned 6 caps for Scotland.
Scotland.managerialCareer: Managed West Bromwich Albion, Reading and Kilmarnock before taking over Scotland in 2019, qualifying for Euro 2020 and Euro 2024 and reaching the 2026 finals.
Scotland.bio: Steve Clarke is Scotland's longest-serving modern manager, in charge since 2019. After ending long tournament droughts with back-to-back Euros qualifications, he has now led Scotland to the 2026 finals.

Senegal.name: Pape Thiaw
Senegal.playingCareer: Forward who played for clubs including AS Saint-Étienne, RC Strasbourg and Deportivo Alavés, earning 16 caps and 5 goals for Senegal and featuring at the 2002 finals.
Senegal.managerialCareer: Coached ASC Niarry Tally and Senegal's home-based side, winning the 2022 African Nations Championship, before being named senior head coach in December 2024 and reaching the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations final.
Senegal.bio: Pape Thiaw is a former Senegal forward who rose through the federation's ranks to take the senior job in 2024. He led the Lions of Teranga to a 2025 Africa Cup of Nations final and qualification for the 2026 tournament.

South Africa.name: Hugo Broos
South Africa.playingCareer: Defender who won multiple league titles with Anderlecht and Club Brugge and earned 24 caps for Belgium between 1974 and 1986.
South Africa.managerialCareer: Won Belgian championships as a manager with Club Brugge and famously led Cameroon to the 2017 Africa Cup of Nations title before taking charge of South Africa in 2021.
South Africa.bio: Hugo Broos is a veteran Belgian coach who masterminded Cameroon's surprise 2017 Africa Cup of Nations triumph. Since 2021 he has rebuilt South Africa around a young core and guided Bafana Bafana to the 2026 finals.

Spain.name: Luis de la Fuente
Spain.playingCareer: Left-back who spent most of his career at Athletic Bilbao, with spells at Sevilla and Deportivo Alavés.
Spain.managerialCareer: Long-serving coach within Spain's youth setup who won the 2015 U19 and 2019 U21 European titles before taking the senior side in 2022, winning Euro 2024 and the 2023 UEFA Nations League.
Spain.bio: Luis de la Fuente rose through Spain's youth ranks, winning European titles at U19 and U21 level before being promoted to the senior job. He delivered a Nations League trophy and the Euro 2024 crown, and leads La Roja into the 2026 tournament as one of the favourites.

Sweden.name: Graham Potter
Sweden.playingCareer: Left-back who made over 300 senior appearances for clubs including Birmingham City, Stoke City, Southampton and West Bromwich Albion.
Sweden.managerialCareer: Made his name at Östersund in Sweden (2017 Svenska Cupen) before managing Swansea City, Brighton & Hove Albion, Chelsea and West Ham United; appointed Sweden manager in 2025.
Sweden.bio: Graham Potter built his reputation guiding unfancied Östersund up the Swedish leagues and into Europe, then earned admiration for his work at Brighton in the Premier League. He returned to Sweden as national-team boss in late 2025 and steered the side to the 2026 finals via the playoffs.

Switzerland.name: Murat Yakin
Switzerland.playingCareer: Centre-back who played for Grasshoppers, VfB Stuttgart, Fenerbahçe and FC Basel, winning multiple Swiss titles, and won 49 caps for Switzerland.
Switzerland.managerialCareer: Managed several Swiss clubs including FC Basel, with whom he won two Swiss Super League titles; appointed Switzerland head coach in 2021 and led the team to the 2022 finals and the Euro 2024 quarter-finals.
Switzerland.bio: Murat Yakin was a combative defender and Swiss international before turning to coaching, winning back-to-back league titles at boyhood club Basel. As national-team boss since 2021 he has overseen a consistent run of qualifications and reached the Euro 2024 quarter-finals.

Tunisia.name: Hervé Renard
Tunisia.playingCareer: Lower-league defender in France whose clubs included AS Cannes; he did not play at the top professional level.
Tunisia.managerialCareer: Two-time Africa Cup of Nations winner (Zambia 2012, Ivory Coast 2015) who also managed Morocco, Saudi Arabia and the France women's team; appointed Tunisia head coach in June 2026.
Tunisia.bio: Hervé Renard is one of the most decorated coaches in African football, having lifted the Africa Cup of Nations with both Zambia and Ivory Coast and masterminded Saudi Arabia's famous win over Argentina at the 2022 finals. He took charge of Tunisia in June 2026, just ahead of the tournament.

Türkiye.name: Vincenzo Montella
Türkiye.playingCareer: Prolific striker nicknamed 'Aeroplanino' who starred for Roma and also played for Sampdoria, Genoa and Empoli, winning Serie A in 2001 and earning 20 caps for Italy.
Türkiye.managerialCareer: Managed Fiorentina, AC Milan (2016 Supercoppa Italiana), Sampdoria, Sevilla and Adana Demirspor before taking charge of Turkey in 2023, reaching the Euro 2024 quarter-finals.
Türkiye.bio: Vincenzo Montella was a sharp, left-footed striker best remembered for his goalscoring exploits at Roma. As a coach he has worked across Italy, Spain and Turkey, and since 2023 has revitalised the Turkish national side, reaching the Euro 2024 quarter-finals and guiding them to the 2026 finals.

USA.name: Mauricio Pochettino
USA.playingCareer: Centre-back who played for Newell's Old Boys, Espanyol, Paris Saint-Germain and Bordeaux, winning two Copa del Rey titles, and earned 20 caps for Argentina including the 2002 finals.
USA.managerialCareer: Managed Espanyol, Southampton, Tottenham (2019 Champions League final), Paris Saint-Germain (2022 Ligue 1) and Chelsea before taking the United States job in 2024 and reaching the 2025 Gold Cup final.
USA.bio: Mauricio Pochettino is a globally respected manager who guided Tottenham to a Champions League final and won Ligue 1 with Paris Saint-Germain. Appointed to lead the United States in 2024, he brings elite pedigree to the host nation's bid at the 2026 tournament.

Uruguay.name: Marcelo Bielsa
Uruguay.playingCareer: Defender who played briefly for Newell's Old Boys and lower-division clubs in Argentina before retiring at 25 to focus on coaching.
Uruguay.managerialCareer: Influential tactician who managed Argentina (2004 Olympic gold), Chile, Athletic Bilbao, Marseille and Leeds United (2020 promotion) before becoming Uruguay head coach in 2023, finishing third at the 2024 Copa América.
Uruguay.bio: Marcelo Bielsa, nicknamed 'El Loco', is one of football's most influential and intense tacticians, admired by a generation of top coaches. Since taking over Uruguay in 2023 he has rebuilt the side around attacking, high-pressing football, finishing third at the 2024 Copa América.

Uzbekistan.name: Fabio Cannavaro
Uzbekistan.playingCareer: World-class centre-back who played for Napoli, Parma, Inter, Juventus and Real Madrid, won 136 caps for Italy, and captained the 2006 title-winning side while claiming the 2006 Ballon d'Or.
Uzbekistan.managerialCareer: Managed Guangzhou Evergrande, Al Nassr, the China national team, Benevento, Udinese and Dinamo Zagreb before being appointed Uzbekistan head coach in October 2025.
Uzbekistan.bio: Fabio Cannavaro is an Italian football legend, the 2006 Ballon d'Or winner who captained Italy to glory that summer. Now in management, he took charge of Uzbekistan in late 2025 as the Central Asian nation prepares for its first appearance at the 2026 tournament.
